payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
On July 10, 2026, IFPI and RIAA led a coalition announcing a shared system for labeling generative AI in sound recordings. A2IM, WIN, IMPALA, the Recording Academy, SAG-AFTRA, and the Human Artistry Campaign stand behind it. Two icons. An uppercase AI on black for recordings where a generative system produced the whole thing, or the lead vocal, or a key instrumental part. A lowercase ai on white for recordings made substantially by people with generative help somewhere in the process.

The taxonomy is sound. Both categories describe things that exist, and the line between them falls about where a working producer would draw it. A2IM's statement placed provenance next to authenticity as the ground that trust actually stands on, which is not a word trade bodies reach for casually.

The labels are voluntary. They are built for adoption by digital services and distributors, none of whom signed the announcement.

So the open question is not whether the categories are right. It is who ends up wearing them.

What a label asserts

A label is a metadata field. Someone sets it at upload, usually a distributor acting on what a client told them. Nothing ties the field to the audio. Edit the field and the recording is untouched. A service receiving a tagged track receives a claim it has no way to test, and a service receiving an untagged track receives silence.

Silence is where the weight lands. Once a labeling scheme exists, the absence of a label stops being neutral. It becomes an implicit statement that no generative system was involved, issued by nobody in particular and backed by nothing. The scheme's credibility rests on the honesty of the set it never touches.

The cost falls on whoever tells the truth

The coalition's own announcement supplies the numbers. Deezer has reported AI-generated tracks running at 44 percent of new music arriving on the platform. Apple Music has reported that more than a third of its tracks are entirely AI.

Ask who fills in the field.

A label carries consequences for whoever applies it. Editorial playlists and listeners both respond to it, and neither response favors the labeled track. The producer who dropped a generative pad under a bridge pays a price for saying so. The account uploading four hundred prompted tracks a month pays nothing for saying nothing. Any system that charges for candor and gives silence away free will collect most of its labels from the people who were never the problem.

Tidal shifted the obligation onto distributors in June, which relocates the honor system without closing it. Deezer at least does not depend on the uploader, since it tags from its own detection model, and it inherits everything that comes with scoring instead of proving.

Nothing can prove an absence

"No generative AI was used here" is a claim about the entire space of things that did not happen. No evidence establishes it. There is no artifact and no signature that can be produced to demonstrate that something was never done. The producer accused of feeding a vocal through a model cannot clear the accusation. The producer who actually did it will never be caught by the labeling system, because the labeling system only reads what people chose to write in it.

Turn the claim around and it becomes tractable. That a person holding a particular key performed these takes on this date, and that the stems they assembled arrived from named sources, are positive statements about events that occurred. A record of them can exist. It has to be made while the work is being made, which is the whole difficulty and also the whole answer. No icon retrofits onto a finished file what was never captured during the session that produced it.

There is a second difference, and it decides adoption. Labeling asks for an extra act from the party least motivated to perform it, while a record made during the session costs nothing at the moment of honesty because it accrues as a byproduct of doing the work at all.

The announcement says the labeling is designed to evolve as technology and requirements change. Read that as an admission. The people who wrote it know what is missing.

The recordings that most need a label are the ones that will never carry one.
payload.title: The unlabeled majority
